author: @GrumblingGrognard
text: Hobbyist/Enthusiast vs. "end-user"

author: @Splarkszter
text:
Yes, if you are someone that just wants to print, best thing for everyone is that you get the most recyclable filament you can, so you can minimize waste and contamination, besides that nothing much more, thingiverse is pretty much all you need.

On the other hand, as a real tool (and specifically for prototyping), you have to know your printer and filament capabilities, that will 100% make the difference on wasting hours and lots of money instead of just spending hours.

author: @d1v1s1onby0
text:
@Robert Murray-Smith 
I have a suggestion for a video I'd love to see and believe fits with your genre.
I don't believe 3d printing is an end-stage solution in all cases, I constantly get asked things like "How can my prints be made stronger" and "How can I print things that won't melt or degrade with heat or sunlight" 
Some companies such as PCBway (Doesn't have to them for this, it's just an example) offer a service where you can do your rapid prototyping until you get a part that fits and then upload the file to them and they will quote on having it machined in a material of your choice like aluminium or steel.
I'd love to see you design a couple of basic test pieces and run through the process eventually reviewing the product you get back - do the sizes match are the holes in the right place, threaded correctly. That kind of thing (Even just some brackets with a few holes, some threaded etc).
I think this would be really interesting for people using a 3d printer as a stepping stone to their end item. 
Maybe you could even get PCWaby to sponsor it? but that may mean they take extra care with your job and create false expectations.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: now you might see why I am so hell bent for leather on designing upgrades that will allow the 3D printer to manufacture things on par with what people think of when you say "replicator" like printing out actual LEDs as in the part that generates the light, as well as the metal connectors and plastic housing to contain it all and shape the light out the front. or the little layers that make the gates in a CPU so you can print out any idea for a CPU or ram you want. :) (as well as using that same tech but on steriods to break down stuff and make nano particles you want for printing with. so you can reclaim material from old unwanted items, making the crushed electronics vein in a dump site into a gold mine for people who have these recycling systems.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: basically imagine a 3D printer you have on a desktop that you could feed in old busted cell phones and then download a new cell phone design into it and it prints out that new phone using the material from the old busted phones you fed it earlier :)

author: @netikras
text:
I remember the days when Rob was all about "look around, how much stuff people are throwing out and how easy it is to reuse it and bring it back to its second life". 
Nowadays Rob's more like "get a 3d printer and filament and just print the thing yourself".

IDK why, but it makes me kind of sad. Probably because "current Rob"'s approach sort of contradicts the "old Rob"'s approach. Unless there is a way to make DIY filament out of failed (or successful) prints and reuse them...? Or use throwaway products (PET bottles, PP buckets, etc.) to make your own filament? That would be more like the "old Rob" - reusing and recycling, cleaning up the environment and also making something useful out of it!

author: @BASEDinMaine
text:
We have some great 3D printers at my school's electrical automation program.  I wanted to use one for a simple mount for a motor circuit lab, but I was confronted with the initial learning curve of the software.  Totally learnable as all software is, but it was for something very simple and a time-sensitive assignment.  I went with cardboard and duct tape instead 😅

I went with Blender as it's open-source and plenty of tutorials, but it was still going to be a few days before I would have had something working.  Sounds like TinkerCAD may have been a bit more intuitive for beginners.

author: @themeek351
text:
This is a bit off topic, but I couldn't help myself!  I constantly had to clean the carbon build-up on my wood stoves' window and from a bit of knowledge gained from Robert I thought of the simple solution of placing a neodymium magnet at the edge of the window.  Making sure the correct side of the magnet is facing towards the window it repelled most of the carbon, while leaving a beautiful magnetic field pattern on the window!  Now I can enjoy my 🔥 again!  Thanks for all you do Robert!
Oh, and yes I love 3d printing too and have been doing it since 2015!

author: @paulreinhardt6052
text: This is definitely true for filament printers. Resin printers, while producing incredible detail, are still much more difficult. Supports are significantly harder. The print, clean, uv cycle is messy and not great for you. If you just want to tinker, stick with filament for now.

author: @skampp
text: I mostly agree.  The tech is far enough along that you don't need to know too much.  But to purchase a printer that over the course of a year, will never give a failed print for a reason is not realistic.  Eventually you will have to expand your knowledge just a little bit, albeit not enough to put you off from buying one.  Also early on, you should find that there are about 5 or 6 settings that you do need to understand if you are printing anything intricate, or anything that needs strength in certain orientations, and to make sure it doesn't print a bird's nest.  Aside from that, experience will drive your need to learn and you can be happy with your hammer for the most part.  It's not really unlike making a generator.  It will output power if you have only a little basic understanding, sure.  It was rocket science a hundred years ago but not so much now.  But if you really want it to be the best you can make it, you'll have to put in the hours.

author: @philipatkinson1532
text:
I also frequently think of a hammer when using my 3D printer, I’ve managed to avoid hitting it very, very hard so far (my second), but only just. It works fine for a few months and then prints spaghetti, clogs, clicks like a maniac while not really feeding at the right rate and other irritating habits. I have looked at many YouTube channels on the subject, and there are some excellent resources there. I’ve learnt far more than I would have wished, but, in the end, and to my huge embarrassment as someone working within science, it’s moody. If I refuse to talk to it for a couple of months it often then behaves.  Clearly absurd, nothing happens without reason, obviously my fault, but I just don’t have that degree of tolerance. 
I’m now even more skilled at swearing than before it’s acquisition, not sure this is a strong selling point really.
Whilst commenting - recently found your channel.. love it, your talk about capacitors, super capacitors and batteries - thank heavens. There are still people who think. People not afraid of complexity, end enthusiasts. 
Strong work :)

author: @olymk2

replies.author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
replies.text: The main problem with plastics is that there is many parts of the ecosystem where it accumulates. If you want an easy solution to plastic waste, demand that it is incinerated in efficient incinerators instead of it ending up in the biosphere. If done right, burning most kinds of plastics is not more harmful than other carbon based fuel sources.

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: The main plastic used in 3D printing is called PLA and is actually made from food products. (corn starch etc) so it's not a oil based plastic like the other stuff you see. It also is biodegradable under the right circumstances.

replies.author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
replies.text: @DKFX1 The right circumstances is however, not here on planet earth, you need high pressure and temperatures for PLA to degrade, what actually happens here on planet earth is that PLA eventually gets turned into microplastics, it never really degrades, not on this planet. Also the precursor used for the polymer does not predict the properties or the longevity of the final product.

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: @ET_AYY_LMAO You can decompose PLA in industrial composting facilities in a few weeks. Major advantage over traditional oil derived plastics.

replies.author: @olymk2
replies.text: @DKFX1 unfortunately I am not convinced that is the case most of the time but I would love to be proven wrong, I would love a material I could throw on a home compost heap even if eventually I used plastic at least I would know the test print could decompose.

author: @ProlificInvention
text: I have an ancient Wanhao I3 which prints pretty much perfectly, I'm still using CURA as a slicer and thingiverse for most files.  I have printed over 17 miles of filament with it and never needed anything except a heated bed wire protector chain which I 3d printed.  I also printed clips which hold the sheet of glass on the heated bed.  My favorite print is an 18 blade shrouded propeller (parametric) which I have printed many copies in the 200mm diameter size.  Those propellers look like turbine blades and are exceedingly efficient over all others I've tested (which is many)

replies.author: @rosskstar
replies.text: Is it like the new torus shaped props that are appearing on the market?

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text: @rosskstar No, they're very unique.  They look like an actual turbine with a ring of material around the circumference of the 18 blades.  I used them to generate electricity in numerous experiments on the front of my car, my motorcycle, and I even made a fencetop module featuring 12 of them driving 12v 20 watt generator motors.

author: @thebytespider
text:
I think I might have to disagree a little on this one. 3D printers have come along massively in the last few years, making them much easier and more user friendly. You can for the most part just grab a file and print it. 

However, what if you try a new filament and for some reason it's not sticking to the bed, or the layer adhesion is not quite right. Or perhaps the print is curling at the corners or getting knocked off mid-print. Without learning how the printer and the materials work you would find it hard to intuition what the solution might be and may lead to frustration and  you could think 3d printing isn't "ready" yet.

material

Your message to buy a 3d printer and try it to make something is great though, and I wholeheartedly agree!

author: @mikejones-vd3fg
text:
Yeah i dont want to be tweaking my printer constantly  either but without some crazy AI help ive accepted the fact its still part of the process, you still have to aim the hammer striaght, which takes some getting use to at the start, i see printer settings part of the learning curve.  But could do without honestly, an AI should be able to analyze the model and give the best settings and movements to print it without fail.   But until then I can see why some would rather make a 3d printer then print, the industry is so new and evolving, lots of room for improvement. And with all its innovatoin a guy who makes 3d printers out of 3d printed parts and 8 bit microcontroller still makes the best in the world... so theres room for more i think.    

But once thats figured out id have to agree with some here, 3d printing isnt the hard part, its making the models, in software thats so unintuative to use.  And thats just the limitations of 2d ,  its not intuititve for us to move 3d objects in 2d, we cant push up or down into the screen, so its a button click fest of re-organizging your view which is where i think VR CAD tools could be a game changer.   They find most people can interact in 3d VR way better then a 2d video game even without experience, we live in 3d afterall, so forming objects in 3d would be so much easer, making a wierd shape with your hands etc.  Ive seen some cool 3d artist tools for VR,  CAD is begging for such a thing.

replies.author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
replies.text:
What printer do you have? I never tweak anything on my printer really, I just print and it works, sure sometimes I tweak print settings if I have some specific requirements in mind like weight or strength.
Of course you have to either design with 3d printing in mind, or use supports to have the print be succesful.

replies.author: @mikejones-vd3fg
replies.text: @ET_AYY_LMAO Oh yeah, thats cool, whch one do you have? I dont have one yet partly because I dont know which one wont have me upgrading and tweaking, i settled on an artillery hornet that didnt come through on aliexpress so im back to square 1 shopping and stuck in option paralysis hah any recommendations are welcome.   In the meantime trying to figure out how to get models idea efficiently into cad id rather spend my time tweaking with that then with printers, so im thinking of just going witha  Prusa mini, although even those i heard arent immune as one print farmer was saying there werne reliable enough for his use anyway until he made some upgrades.  And i still just cant believe theres nothing close to reliable for a more budget freindly price but i havent found anything, that doenst require upgrades anyway.  Other than that im leaning toward a cr6 se or an anycubic vyper for not much more I can pick up here localy.  But then someone has to throw a curve ball and posts a video saying the cr6 is betteer then the vyper, just because he had bad customer service and it was hard to get parts? I asked if he didnt have issues with customer service and nothing broke would he still recommend the cr6 over the vyper? he said yes because he had to order parts for the cr6 and it was easy install and fast ordering, but that doesnt bode well for the quality of c6 if he's already ordering parts for it... and the vyper could have been a one off thing,  i dunno...

replies.author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
replies.text:
@mikejones-vd3fg The one I have now is a Prusa MK3S+, it has been running for two years without much hickup. I am not sure if it is still worth it, to go with Prusa, as the new printers from Bambulab really makes all other printers on the market seem obsolete.

The thing with all the cheap bedslingers from aliexpress is that they usually need a good bit of tweaking to give good results, so if you want something no fuss, I would not recommend just buying any bedslinger printer on deal from aliexpress, but maybe figure out a little about the printer before you buy, like if there is provided good default settings for cura or prusaslicer, if users are generally happy with the printer etc..

If you have the cash, go for Bambulab, for sure. That is what I can see right now.

My old man just bought a printer from aliexpress, a cheap bed slinger with a huge printbed, it prints decently, but there was not provided any default configuration for cura and the version of cura that was supplied was ancient and very much out of date.

replies.author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
replies.text: @mikejones-vd3fg What I really like about the Prusa MK3 is that bed levelling is basically a thing of the past on that machine. You set it up once per plate you have for the machine and it just works every time until you have to change the hotend or something like that. The bed does not have levelling screws at all, its done with sensors and software and thats very nice compared to other bedslingers, you can however get probes for other printers, like BL touch, but I found that Prusas work really reliably (the MK3 dont know about mini).

author: @lv_woodturner3899
text:
I purchased my 3D printer back in Feb 2018.   I wanted this to be a tool to make things like you are doing.    It did not take long for my first print failures.   I then had to learn how to diagnose and fix.   I have two friends with 3D printers.    They also want their printers to be a tool.   Both said they do not have problems with prints - until they have the first hiccup.   One of the friends managed to last 2 years before he had his first print failure which looked like an extruder failure, but it was due to a clogged nozzle.   I end up being the tech support for my friends which is not a problem.   I wish a 3D printer was as easy to use as the old VCR.   It starts to be as easy but my experience is that over time some prints will fail and at that point folks either need to have a friend with more knowledge, or they try forums etc. to attempt to get a diagnosis and solution. 
In January I used my printer for the first time in months.   I noticed an issue with the first layer stands not bonding.    I tried all sorts of tweaks based on information from videos etc.    I ended up finding out that my nozzle had too much internal friction.    The coating on the inside had worn over the 3+ years I had used the nozzle.   I replaced with a new coated nozzle and voila, now my first layer was printing well.  
I just completed my latest print a few minutes ago.   The first layer is slightly warped.    I know this is due to the shape and I tried to print without a brim.   I am now reprinting with a brim to get this to stay flat against the print bed.
I love having a 3D printer and recommend these to my friends but few have gone down this rabbit hole.
Dave.

author: @senorjp21
text: A 5 start tip I got from Ivan Miranda was to wash the build plate with soap and water, and then don't touch it with your fingers. My adhesion problems have been 100% gone since I learned this trick.

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: There is a fine line between Milling and 3D printing.  Computer Aided Manufacturing does include 3D printing and also includes the subtractive manufacturing with lathes and mills.  A mill is basically a 3D printer with a cutter head instead of a ' hot end ' adding material.  With that said, that is why I started into 3D printing, I wanted to make things that you can't just drop down to a shop and buy.  It expanded to getting a table top mill.  If you always keep in mind how the head of either can hit the part and ruin the work, the skills from one machine work well with the other and have more alike than one would have guessed.

author: @allandart20
text: I’ve been able to save a lot of money printing parts for a homebuilt cnc, which in turn will be able to route out parts to improve the cnc once it’s up and running.  Additionally cnc is very similar to 3d printing, the difference between is one adds material and the other takes away material.  Cnc can be made very cheaply by using arduino. Once you are familiar with how this process works then you are only limited in your imagination with what to built.

replies.author: @TheWingnut58
replies.text:
Don't leave out a furnace for aluminum casting so you can use the 3d printer to make patterns and cast parts for an even more capable cnc....
This is a never ending  addiction,  lol

author: @angelusmendez5084
text: YES 😎🎉

author: @cayrex
text: Well said

author: @matbritton6816
text: The future is 3d printing androids. Biodegradable materials would be good. Hemp fibre or hemp graphine is pretty good option.

author: @CCoburn3
text: It wasn't that long ago that A 3D printer wasn't worth buying. The only things people were making with them were toys and parts/accessories for 3D printers. But as you say, all that has changed. Plus, the prices of 3D printers and filament have dropped significantly. And when your 3D printer isn't busy working on something you designed, there's a world of designs available on places like Thingiverse. A 3D printer is certainly a worthwhile investment now.

author: @jerrodlopes186
text: I've been on the fence for years, but finally started to tip toward getting one. I have to say that the companies that manufacture these things could do themselves a favor and advertise them someway other than just making toys with them. To each his own, but I couldn't care less about figurines and the like. I would dearly love to see one outfitted for making wax or foam models for lost wax casting. I'm still trying to decide whether I would end up actually using the thing or if it would just end up on the table next to my unused laser cutter.

author: @saiello2061
text: My company bought a small 3D printer ( +£1000! ) a decade ago for exclusive use by R&D dept. I knew what one looked like, never seen one in the flesh. It was closely guarded but I managed to wrangle some time on it. It opened up my engineering endeavours at the company like you wouldn't believe. Changed the whole nature of prototyping for me and opened up new ways of thinking about solving problems. Whether you're an engineer, designer, artist, maker, etc, a 3D printer is a no brainer! Bought my own several years ago and haven't looked back, if only they were around when I was a kid..!  Every primary school should have a 3D printer and kids should be taught to use them and the design software to go with it. It's as revolutionary as the lathe was. If not for yourself and you're a parent, don't deprive your kids, just do it! 😁

replies.author: @tomislavruzicic3955
replies.text: Well, if you compare 3d printer with lathe then we are goning to see new industrial revolution :) Joking aside, I too have 3d printer and for a small foundry is a must have... I made many test models for casting. A beautiful machine

author: @AncientTechDIYChannel
text: 3D printers are far from being a good tool. I tried one several years ago and got a new one just a few months ago and I am really not impressed. It's still just a glorified glue gun. The software for it is still the same, the hardware has hardly changed and it still has the same dumb problems it did 5 years ago. I actually think there is a conspiracy to keep 3D printers from becoming accurate. A sensor providing feed back of the print in combination with an evolutionary algorithm would solve most problems but it's not being done.... why? Nobody thought of it except me?

author: @newmonengineering
text: I have 3d printers, cnc and laser cutter. 3d printers can produce very unique items. Cnc is great when you need some aluminum parts, or wood parts. Laser is for fast sheet wood or decorative engravings like acrylic, glass or wood. I find the laser the easiest to use and have it turn out right simple 2d drawing and a few settings. 3d printers are a bit more involved because you have to understand slicer settings and design 3d in CAD, but can make the most unique things. Cnc is also a bit more involved because you have to learn a bit of CAM so it carves correctly. All that said, I have many miles on all 3 machines. I tend to use laser and 3d printer more than cnc. Laser is the fastest of them.

author: @G1gop
text: I am undecided about a 3D printer. All I want are cases for projects but a lot of the time they don't exist. So I have to start modifying boxes.  I am NOT interested in the CAD side of things at all though.Thats the side that puts me off. So how much for a suitable 3D printer? What do they cost to run(I here of 19 hrs printing etc!). I looked at firms to do the 3D printing for me but the cost is ridiculous for a 'one off project box' (example pi zero with  extra board with access to all sockets.

replies.author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
replies.text: You can get decent printer starting at 200usd, I wouldn't go much lower than that. There is lots of offers out there, I know some places have Ender-3's for like 99usd.

author: @deanallenjones
text:
I've been printing for 10 years and my machine is running all the time. Sometimes it's models and terrain for Dungeons and Dragons, other times it's household tools. (eg, just printed a new litter picker, instead of having something made in china and shipped to an amazon Wearhouse and then to me)
BUT Robert is right about learning a little modelling. I've  fixed so many things around the house just by using tinkercad.

author: @paprjam
text: I bought a cheap e-bay printer and didn't know what I was doing. That was a disaster. Made it very hard to get a good print and was discouraging to say the least. Now I'm saving my pennies to get a better machine and learning CAD.

author: @dragasan
text: Yeah, along as the tool works as intended, I'm all in, but when it doesn't, I need to know how improve upon it.

author: @EastBayFlipper
text:
In the 90's I was in an engineering work term and the company i was working for had a unigraphics workstation connected to a cnc mill and lathe to rapid prototype new designs to assess fit and finish.
I couldn't even guess what it cost but LOTS is a good guess.
..and now, a lot of what was done could have been replaced with a 3d printer for initial concept presentation, saving a lot of man hours. 🤔

author: @In.Darkness
text:
I just want to turn the damn lights on ! LOL

Great work thanks!

author: @grendel1960a
text:
well I can say I am in full agreement with you here, when I started 3d printing i spent a small fortune to buy a fully ready out of the box, no assembly required printer, this tied me in to using the manufacturers filament cartridges - but because these (expensive) cartridges had microchips, they controlled everything, filament temperature, bed temperature and all the other parameters, this meant that you could throw a file at them in the knowledge that it would just work.
I am lucky, I ma that person with extensive CAD knowledge, I could knock up my designs in CAD and then just print them, all I needed was the tool to do the printing, I wasnt interested in how it did it, the settings it used etc, i just wanted my design. we are probably talking 6 years ago, when you spent days building the printer, then it didnt just work without fiddling, that wasnt for me. 
nowadays with modern printers that take half a dozen screws to assemble and just work, where all the default settings are in the cura brand that comes with the printer, and you get your file and hit go, that was the experience I was looking for those 6 years ago- 
in those 6 years my stl file has expanded to nearly 1000 items that I have designed and printed, I now mostly use my small flsun printer to print stuff, that said I did fire up my da vinci printer the other day, as I wanted an ABS part to repair a broken bracket on the case of my tablet, the da vinci is an enclosed case machine, so handles ABS much better (and I was already using the flsun on a 4 hour print) so within the hour i had designed and printed the necessary spare part .

author: @staycurious8650
text: I've been a hammer and spanner man myself, 30+ years heavy industry mechanical engineering and maintenance but the coding bug bit me couple of years ago, love it. Who new one could create and use non physical tools as well.  All my published apps on Google play store are tools. Also created a few that I've used in my work, like measuring the length of conveyor belt to replace, using gps and and a little math. 💯🛠

author: @whosle
